Given all this, the most straightforward diagnosis is hypoglycemia, possibly from an endocrine cause or an external factor like medication or alcohol, though those details aren't provided.

The patient presents with hypoglycemia (35 mg/dL) and a normal Hgb A1c (5.1%), indicating no chronic hyperglycemia.
The decreased C-peptide suggests low endogenous insulin production.
Since hypoglycemia with low C-peptide implies an external insulin source, the most likely cause is exogenous insulin administration.

This could be accidental or intentional (factitious hypoglycemia), especially given her recent stress from a divorce, which might have led to emotional distress or access to insulin (e.g., from her mother, who has diabetes).
Insulinomas are excluded due to the low C-peptide (they would cause elevated C-peptide).
Other causes like adrenal insufficiency or non-β-cell tumors are less likely given the lab results and lack of supporting symptoms.
The acute nature and normal Hgb A1c make insulin overdose the most plausible diagnosis.
